questions.q: What does the fundamental attribution error refer to?
options: The tendency to overestimate internal factors and underestimate external factors when explaining others' behavior
The tendency to blame ourselves for others' misfortunes
The inability to make accurate attributions about behavior
The preference for making global rather than specific attributions
questions.explanation: The fundamental attribution error causes people to attribute others' behavior to personality or disposition while ignoring situational factors. In contrast, people tend to attribute their own negative behavior to circumstances.

options: The tendency to attribute others' behavior to their personality while attributing our own to situations
The tendency to behave differently when being observed
The tendency to observe behavior without reacting to it
The preference for observational learning over direct experience
questions.explanation: The actor-observer bias reflects opposite attributional patterns: we attribute others' behavior to their disposition but our own behavior to external circumstances. This is related to the fundamental attribution error but emphasizes perspective differences.

questions.q: In Asch's conformity experiments, approximately what percentage of trials did participants give obviously incorrect answers to match the group?
questions.explanation: In Asch's line judgment task, participants conformed to obviously wrong group answers about 30-35% of the time. This classic finding demonstrates the powerful influence of group pressure on conformity.

options: The discomfort experienced when holding contradictory attitudes or beliefs
The tendency to forget information that contradicts our worldview
The inability to process conflicting information simultaneously
The preference for harmony in groups despite logical inconsistencies
questions.explanation: Cognitive dissonance is the psychological discomfort from holding contradictory cognitions. People are motivated to reduce this dissonance by changing attitudes, beliefs, or behaviors to restore consistency.

questions.q: What is the risky shift phenomenon?
options: Groups tend to make riskier decisions than individuals typically would
People avoid risks when in groups
Risk tolerance decreases with group size
Group decisions are always more conservative than individual decisions
questions.explanation: The risky shift demonstrates that groups often make riskier decisions than their individual members would alone. This occurs through group polarization where discussion amplifies initial tendencies.

questions.q: What does the bystander effect suggest about helping behavior?
options: People are less likely to help when others are present
Bystanders always help in emergency situations
The presence of others increases helping behavior
Familiarity with others determines helping behavior
questions.explanation: The bystander effect shows that people are less likely to help in emergencies when other people are present. Diffusion of responsibility—the belief that others will help—reduces individual likelihood of intervention.

options: The enhancement of performance on simple tasks and impairment on complex tasks in the presence of others
The tendency to like people who are similar to us
The ability to work well in group settings
The improvement of performance in all tasks when others are present
questions.explanation: Social facilitation describes how an audience enhances performance on well-learned tasks (dominant responses) but impairs performance on complex or newly learned tasks. The arousal caused by observation is key to this effect.

options: The tendency to favor members of one's own group and view them more positively
The tendency to view all group members as similar
The preference for joining groups with similar members
The likelihood of conforming within a group setting
questions.explanation: In-group bias causes people to attribute positive qualities to their own group members and view out-group members less favorably. This bias occurs even in arbitrary or minimal group situations.

questions.q: According to social impact theory, what three factors influence the likelihood of conformity?
options: Strength, immediacy, and number of group members
Intelligence, personality, and motivation
Age, gender, and cultural background
Experience, education, and socioeconomic status
questions.explanation: Latané's social impact theory identifies three key factors: strength (how important the group is), immediacy (physical closeness), and number (size of the group). Greater values on these dimensions increase conformity pressure.

questions.q: What is the false consensus effect?
options: The tendency to overestimate the extent to which others share our beliefs and behaviors
The belief that consensus cannot be achieved in groups
The tendency to change our opinions to match group consensus
The difficulty in identifying what most people actually believe
questions.explanation: The false consensus effect leads people to believe their own opinions and behaviors are more common and normal than they actually are. This bias makes our views seem more shared than data typically supports.
